    • Problem 1: Language Model Perplexity

Perplexity

What is Perplexity?

Perplexity is a measurement of how well a probability model predicts a sample. It is an exponentiated version of entropy (or cross-entropy) that transforms the logarithmic scale of bits back into a more intuitive, linear scale of “equivalent choices.”

If a model has a perplexity of \(K\), it means the model is, on average, as “confused” or “perplexed” as if it had to guess uniformly among \(K\) equally likely options.

Mathematical Definition

For a discrete probability distribution \(P\), the perplexity is defined as \(2\) raised to the power of the Shannon Entropy (if the entropy is calculated in bits):

\[ PP(P) = 2^{H(P)} = 2^{-\sum x P(x) \log_2 P(x)} \]

If the natural logarithm (nats) is used, perplexity is exponentiated using \(e\): \[ PP(P) = e^{H(P)} \]

Example

Suppose a model is predicting the outcome of a fair 6-sided die. The entropy is \(\log_2(6) \approx 2.58\) bits. The perplexity is \(2^{2.58} = 6\). This matches our intuition perfectly: the model is facing \(6\) equally likely choices. If a biased die model achieves a perplexity of \(3.5\), it means the model is as confident as if it were choosing between \(3.5\) equally likely options.

Perplexity of a Model (Cross-Entropy Perplexity)

In practice, we don’t calculate the perplexity of the true distribution; we calculate the perplexity of a model \(Q\) given a true test sequence \(x_1, \dots, x_N\) drawn from the true distribution \(P\).

This is simply the exponentiated Cross-Entropy \(H(P, Q)\):

\[ PP(Q) = 2^{H(P, Q)} = 2^{ -\frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^N \log_2 Q(x_i) } \]

Machine Learning Context: NLP Language Models

Perplexity is the gold-standard evaluation metric for Language Models (LMs) like GPT or BERT.

A language model assigns probabilities to sequences of words. When evaluating an LM on a held-out test dataset of \(N\) words, we calculate the perplexity of the model’s predictions.

Interpreting LM Perplexity

If a language model achieves a perplexity of 15 on a test corpus, it means that at every step of generating a sentence, the model is as confused as if it had to choose uniformly among 15 possible next words.

  • Lower is Better: A perplexity of 1 means perfect prediction (absolute certainty).
  • A model predicting words completely at random from a vocabulary of \(50,000\) words would have a perplexity of \(50,000\).
  • State-of-the-art Large Language Models (LLMs) often achieve single-digit perplexities on standard English corpora, demonstrating a profound understanding of grammar, context, and semantics.

Practice Problems

Problem 1: Language Model Perplexity

A language model assigns a uniform probability over a vocabulary of \(10,000\) words. What is its perplexity? If a better model achieves a perplexity of \(50\), what does this physically mean in terms of its predictive power?
